formulation: Your noble lie required rulers who knew the good the fiction served. Krastev defended the hypocrisy of the international order as a necessary fiction protecting the weak from pure force — but no one at Munich could say what truth stands behind this fiction, and the powers who maintain it cannot distinguish the lie from their own convenience. Can a founding fiction hold a political order together when no one in the order possesses the knowledge that would make the fiction noble rather than merely useful?
context_narrative: At Munich, a panel on the fracturing international order split not on whether the system is hypocritical — everyone conceded that — but on whether the hypocrisy is load-bearing. Ivan Krastev argued that 'something worse than liberal hypocrisy is a world with no hypocrisy at all,' defending the fiction of sovereign equality as the only thing standing between small states and naked force. Across the hall, Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez called that same hypocrisy a vulnerability threatening democracies globally, while the US National Security Advisor pitched ad hoc coalitions as a replacement without addressing whether they could protect anyone who lacked the power to be invited. No one proposed what truth — if any — the fiction was meant to serve.

selected_quotes.quote: There is something worse than liberal hypocrisy, and that is a world with no hypocrisy at all — a world where only power matters — because then small countries have no reason to distinguish between the US and China, and American exceptionalism is reduced to being exceptionally powerful.

In a rules-based order, hypocrisy is vulnerability — the West's habit of looking the other way for inconvenient populations undermines the legitimacy of the entire system and threatens democracies globally.

The panic about the breakdown of the rules-based order is a Eurocentric awakening; much of the developing world has lived with might-makes-right for over a decade, and the current conversation is finally catching up to their reality.
selected_quotes.attribution: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud

selected_quotes.quote: The missing element in the international order is accountability: the rules exist and most countries sign up to the UN Charter, but there is no functioning mechanism to hold violators accountable, especially when powerful states breach the principles.

The rules-based international order was always underwritten by power, and recognizing that the US needs to restore its own strength does not mean rejecting all international norms — but rules won't enforce themselves without strong countries behind them.

rationale: Krastev's defense of hypocrisy as necessary fiction maps directly onto Plato's noble lie but inverts its epistemic condition — the fiction has no knower of the good behind it — landing squarely in Plato's activates_on territory (knowledge, authority, the good as knowable rather than merely expedient) while pressing the council fault line on whether structural hypocrisy protects or corrodes.
